moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing Bird Opening, an unusual but principled first move that immediately stakes a claim to the e5-square and prepares to control the long diagonal. Unlike 1.e4 or 1.d4, f4 is less common but gives us creative chances: we support our center with a flank pawn rather than occupying the middle directly, and we keep our position flexible. This move requires good understanding of the resulting positions since Black gets counterplay immediately.

Black strikes right at our center with the natural e5, challenging us to commit to our strategy. By playing e5, Black opens lines and gains space in the middle, but this also allows us to transition into From's Gambit—where we'll push f4 down Black's throat. Black's pawn on e5 is inviting us to gambit, and the position remains sharp and tactical.

We develop our knight to support our center and prepare the key f4 advance (which would be the gambit continuation). Nc3 is flexible: it controls d5 and e4, makes our knight active on a strong square, and keeps our options open. With Nc3, we're not immediately committing to sacrificing on f4, but we're ready to strike when the moment is right while developing naturally.
